旅 Travel Guide Mid-Range · Mix of Everything

Complete 14-Day Itinerary

Two Weeks
in Japan

From Osaka's neon street-food chaos through a floating shrine, ancient Kyoto temples, deer-filled Nara, an alpine Edo-era village, and into the electric heart of Tokyo.

15Days
6Cities
~€130Per Day
~€3,100Total Est.
Osaka + Nara · Days 1–3 Hiroshima / Miyajima · Days 4–5 Kyoto · Days 6–8 Hakone · Days 9–10 Tokyo · Days 11–15
00

Your Route

A clean eastward arc across Honshu — arriving at Kansai International, sweeping west to Hiroshima, looping through Osaka and Nara, rising into the Kyoto temples, pausing for mountain onsen at Hakone (right on the Shinkansen line), and finishing 5 nights in Tokyo. Zero backtracking.

Days 1–3Osaka — Arrive KIX, 3 nights
Day 3Nara — Day trip from Osaka (40 min)
Days 4–5Hiroshima + Miyajima — 2 nights
Days 6–8Kyoto — Ancient capital, 3 nights
Days 9–10Hakone — Onsen & Mt Fuji views, 2 nights
Days 11–15Tokyo — 5 nights, depart Narita/Haneda
Osaka

01

Day-by-Day Itinerary

01 Arrival Day

Arrive KIX → Osaka

  • Land at Kansai International Airport (KIX)
  • Haruka Express to Shin-Osaka or Namba (~75 min)
  • Check in near Namba / Shinsaibashi
  • First evening: Dotonbori food crawl under the neon signs
Eat: Takoyaki, okonomiyaki & kushikatsu on Dotonbori — your introduction to Osaka food culture
02Osaka

Castle, Retro & Street Food

  • Osaka Castle & surrounding moat park (morning)
  • Shinsekai retro 1930s district — try kushikatsu
  • Kuromon Ichiba Market (the "Osaka Kitchen")
  • Shinsaibashi shopping arcade & Amerika-mura
Eat: Snow crab at Kuromon Market · fugu (blowfish) at a Shinsekai specialty restaurant
03 Nara — Day Trip

Deer Park & Giant Buddha

  • Kintetsu from Osaka-Namba → Nara (~40 min)
  • Nara Park — 1,200 free-roaming sacred deer
  • Todai-ji — world's largest bronze Buddha
  • Kasuga Taisha shrine & ancient lantern path
  • Return to Osaka · Kaiyukan Aquarium or evening market
Eat: Kakinoha-zushi (persimmon leaf sushi) — Nara specialty · mochi from Naramachi shops
04 Travel Day

Osaka → Hiroshima

  • Shinkansen Osaka (Shin-Osaka) → Hiroshima (~1h 20m)
  • Peace Memorial Museum — plan 2h minimum (book online)
  • Atomic Bomb Dome (Genbaku Dome) riverside walk
  • Hiroshima Castle grounds afternoon
  • Evening in Nagarekawa entertainment district
Eat: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — layered, not mixed, with noodles inside. Completely different from Osaka style.
05Miyajima

The Floating Torii Gate

  • JR Ferry from Miyajimaguchi → Miyajima (10 min, free with JR Pass)
  • Itsukushima Shrine & the floating torii gate
  • Wild deer roaming freely around the shrine
  • Mt. Misen ropeway — panoramic Seto Inland Sea views
  • Return to Hiroshima · Shinkansen Hiroshima → Kyoto (~2h)
Eat: Grilled oysters straight from the sea · momiji manju (maple-leaf cakes) · evening arrival in Kyoto
06 Travel + Kyoto

→ Kyoto: Fushimi & Gion

  • Check in to Kyoto ryokan or hotel
  • Fushimi Inari Taisha — go at dusk, far fewer crowds than morning
  • Gion district evening walk — spot maiko near Hanamikoji
  • Nishiki Market (the "Kyoto Kitchen") if arriving early enough
Eat: Kaiseki multi-course dinner at your ryokan or a Gion restaurant
07Kyoto

Temples & Gold

  • Kinkaku-ji Golden Pavilion (morning — arrive at opening)
  • Ryoan-ji Zen rock garden
  • Nijo Castle & Ninomaru Palace "nightingale floors"
  • Philosopher's Path evening stroll (Higashiyama)
Eat: Tofu kaiseki near Kinkaku-ji · Kyoto-style ramen (shoyu broth, lighter than Tokyo) at night
08Kyoto

Arashiyama & Bamboo

  • Arashiyama Bamboo Grove (sunrise — before 7 AM for empty shots!)
  • Tenryu-ji Temple garden (UNESCO)
  • Togetsukyo Bridge & monkey park hillside
  • Samurai Ninja Museum or last Kyoto temple of choice
Eat: Matcha parfait in Arashiyama · yudofu (hot pot tofu) dinner near Nanzen-ji
09 Travel Day

Kyoto → Hakone

  • Shinkansen Kyoto → Odawara (~1h 45m) — exit here, not Tokyo
  • Hakone Tozan train or taxi to your ryokan (~20 min)
  • Afternoon at leisure: soak in the private onsen, hike the Owakudani volcanic valley
  • Sunset from Lake Ashi — on a clear day, Mt Fuji framed by the water
Eat: Kaiseki dinner at your ryokan — this is the night to splurge; multi-course mountain cuisine with Hakone produce
10Hakone

Fuji Views, Art & Ropeway

  • Hakone Open Air Museum — world-class sculpture park (Rodin, Picasso, Calder) in mountain forest
  • Hakone Ropeway over Owakudani volcanic crater — black eggs boiled in sulphur springs
  • Lake Ashi cruise with the red torii gate and Mt Fuji backdrop
  • Late afternoon: check out, Romancecar or Shinkansen to Tokyo
Eat: Kurotamago (black sulphur eggs) at Owakudani · Kaiseki breakfast at ryokan · kaiseki lunch near the museum
11 Travel + Tokyo

Hakone → Tokyo

  • Shinkansen Odawara → Tokyo (~35 min) — the fastest leg of the whole trip
  • Check in near Shinjuku, Shibuya, or Asakusa
  • Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden (afternoon)
  • Kabukichō entertainment district & Golden Gai at night
Eat: Ichiran ramen solo dining experience — your first Tokyo meal
12Tokyo

Culture & Harajuku

  • Meiji Jingu Shrine (arrive early — by 7 AM for near-empty grounds)
  • Takeshita Street fashion, crepes & Harajuku subculture
  • Omotesando luxury boutiques & contemporary architecture walk
  • Tokyo Skytree observation deck (sunset or at night)
Eat: Fluffy Japanese pancakes in Omotesando · tempura teishoku dinner in Asakusa
13Tokyo

Asakusa, Akihabara & Shibuya

  • Senso-ji Temple & Nakamise shopping street (early morning)
  • Akihabara anime, manga & electronics district
  • Teamlab Planets — digital art immersion (book in advance!)
  • Shibuya Scramble Crossing at rush hour
Eat: Sushi at Tsukiji Outer Market · wagyu yakiniku in Shibuya at night
14Tokyo

Shimokitazawa, Yanaka & Ginza

  • Shimokitazawa vintage shops, indie cafés & live music venues
  • Yanaka historic neighbourhood — old Tokyo streetscapes, local craft shops
  • Ueno Park & Ameyoko outdoor market
  • Shibuya Sky rooftop at sunset (book ahead!)
Eat: Final Tokyo splurge — omakase sushi in Ginza or soba kaiseki in Yanaka
15 Final Day

Last Hours → Depart

  • Morning walk: Daikanyama / Nakameguro canal (relaxed, photogenic)
  • Last konbini run — stock up on snacks and gifts for the flight
  • Narita Express (N'EX) ~1h or Limousine Bus to Narita · or Keikyu to Haneda (~30 min)
  • Airport ramen at terminal restaurants — one last bowl
Eat: Airport ramen at Narita Terminal 1 Ramen Street · matcha soft serve in the departure hall
★

Worth the Detour?

Six destinations evaluated honestly — transit costs, time trade-offs, and whether they earn their place on a 15-day trip. The mid-trip slot (Days 9–10) has three candidates; the comparison table below the cards gives the head-to-head verdict.

高山
Takayama
Japanese Alps · Gifu · ~3h from Kyoto via Nagoya
↔ Cultural Alternative
Why You Should Go
  • The only place in Japan that genuinely feels like the Edo period — preserved merchant streets with zero modern intrusions
  • Hida beef is exceptional and significantly cheaper than in Tokyo or Osaka
  • Sake brewery tastings are free or near-free; you walk right off the street into them
  • A real palate-cleanser between five days of Tokyo and three days of Kyoto
  • The Hida Limited Express journey through the Japanese Alps is beautiful
The Trade-offs
  • Travel takes ~5h from Tokyo and ~3h onward to Kyoto — two significant half-days lost in transit
  • Two nights is tight — you'll have to choose between Hida Folk Village or the Higashiyama walk, not both
  • Alternatively, these 2 nights could extend Kyoto from 3 to 5 nights — a legitimate choice
Verdict: The most culturally distinct of the three options — but the worst transit value on this particular route. The 3h detour north and 5h return costs more total travel time than Hakone. Choose Takayama if traditional Japan is your priority and you don't mind giving up a half-day extra in transit each way.
宮島
Miyajima
Seto Inland Sea · Off Hiroshima · 10-min ferry
✓✓ Absolute Must
Why You Should Go
  • The floating torii gate is one of Japan's official "Three Views" — genuinely iconic in person
  • Only a 10-min JR ferry from Miyajimaguchi; the ferry is free with your JR Pass
  • Wild deer roam the island completely freely — totally unexpected and magical
  • At high tide it appears to float; at low tide you can walk out and stand beneath it
  • Mt. Misen ropeway gives panoramic views of the Seto Inland Sea
  • Grilled oysters and momiji manju make for one of Japan's best food walks
The Trade-offs
  • Day 5 has Miyajima + an onward Shinkansen to Kyoto — start the ferry by 8 AM for maximum island time
  • Consider staying overnight on Miyajima (Day 5 night) and catching the early Shinkansen to Kyoto Day 6 morning — one of Japan's great sleeps, ~€110–185
Verdict: Non-negotiable — and now it's Day 5, not a rushed final farewell. The only real question is whether to overnight on the island or push on to Kyoto the same evening.
姫路
Himeji Castle
Hyogo Prefecture · 30 min from Osaka by Shinkansen
✓ Worth It (Half-Day)
Why You Should Go
  • Japan's finest surviving original castle — one of only 12 still standing, a UNESCO World Heritage Site
  • Nicknamed "White Heron Castle" for its brilliant white exterior, it dominates the skyline visually in a way no other Japanese castle does
  • Perfectly walkable from Himeji Station — the castle is visible straight down the main boulevard from the moment you exit
  • The adjacent Koko-en garden (nine separate Edo-style gardens) is beautiful and included in combo tickets
  • Only 30 min from Osaka by Shinkansen — the easiest major addition to this itinerary, easy to add en route to Hiroshima on Day 14
  • Interior is genuinely impressive: six floors of original wooden architecture, steep samurai-era staircases, defensive maze layout
The Trade-offs
  • Adding Himeji to Day 14 (Osaka → Himeji → Hiroshima → Miyajima) makes an already full day extremely tight — requires a 6 AM start
  • Better suited as a standalone day trip from Osaka (Day 12 alternative to Nara, or Day 13)
  • Entry fee: ~€6 for castle only; ~€9 with Koko-en combo
  • Can feel rushed in under 2 hours; 3 hours is the comfortable minimum
Verdict: Highly recommended — it's Japan's most architecturally impressive castle, full stop. Best slotted as a day trip from Osaka on Day 12 or 13 rather than tacked onto the already-packed Day 14. If you had to pick one Japanese castle, this is the one.
高野山
Koyasan
Wakayama · 90 min from Osaka · Sacred Buddhist mountain
✓✓ Spiritually Essential
Why You Should Go
  • One of Japan's most sacred sites: a mountain plateau settlement of 117 Buddhist temples founded in 816 AD by Kobo Daishi
  • Okunoin Cemetery — a 2 km forest path through ancient cedar trees and 200,000+ moss-covered stone tombs — is among the most atmospheric places in Japan, especially by lantern-light at night
  • Overnight shukubo (temple lodging) includes a Buddhist vegetarian kaiseki dinner, tatami sleeping rooms, and an early-morning prayer ceremony — an experience unlike anything else on this itinerary
  • The famous Konpon Daito pagoda (vermillion against cedar forest) and Danjo Garan sacred precinct are visually stunning
  • Completely different energy from anywhere else on the trip — meditative, ancient, and genuinely otherworldly
  • Easily accessed from Osaka: Nankai Railway from Osaka-Namba + cable car up the mountain (not on JR Pass, ~€10 each way)
The Trade-offs
  • Requires at minimum one extra night — not feasible as a day trip from Osaka given the travel time and the fact that the experience demands overnight to attend morning prayers
  • Adding it to this 14-day itinerary means sacrificing a night in Osaka or Kyoto — a real cost
  • Temple lodging (shukubo) costs €85–160/person per night including meals — not cheap
  • Buddhist vegetarian cuisine (shojin ryori) is excellent but restrictive — not suitable for everyone
  • No nightlife, limited restaurants outside temple lodging, and very cold in winter
Verdict: Not right for a first 14-day trip unless you deliberately drop a city — but it belongs on almost every second Japan trip. If you can extend to 16 days, replace one Osaka night with a Koyasan overnight. Nothing in Japan offers the same combination of spiritual depth and natural beauty.
箱根
Hakone
Kanagawa · On the Shinkansen line · ~2h from Kyoto
✓✓ Recommended
Why You Should Go
  • Zero routing penalty: Hakone sits directly on the Tokaido Shinkansen corridor between Kyoto and Tokyo — you exit at Odawara (~1h 45m from Kyoto), spend 2 nights, reboard to Tokyo (~35 min). No detour whatsoever.
  • World-class ryokan onsen culture — Hakone is Japan's most developed hot spring resort area, with mineral baths ranging from budget to ultra-luxury
  • Mt Fuji views from Owakudani volcanic valley, Lake Ashi, and the Hakone ropeway — on a clear day it's one of the most dramatic landscapes in Japan
  • Hakone Open Air Museum: internationally renowned sculpture park with outdoor Picassos, a Rodin gallery, and a Stained Glass Forest — genuinely world-class art in mountain nature
  • The Romancecar limited express from Shinjuku is one of Japan's most scenic railway experiences (useful on the Tokyo departure)
  • Strong food scene for the size — Kaiseki ryokan dinners, fresh tofu, and matcha desserts
The Trade-offs
  • One of Japan's most visited resort areas — weekends and Golden Week are extremely crowded; weekday arrival strongly recommended
  • Mt Fuji is cloud-covered 60–70% of the time — don't plan the whole stop around seeing it; treat it as a bonus
  • JR Pass covers Shinkansen to Odawara but NOT the Hakone internal transport (Tozan train, ropeway, boats) — buy the Hakone Free Pass 2-day (€40) at Odakyu counters, which covers everything
  • Ryokan with onsen can be expensive: €120–250/person per night (dinner + breakfast included) — budget options at €65–90 exist near Hakone-Yumoto
  • The ropeway is often closed for volcanic gas safety checks (Owakudani area)
Verdict: The strongest choice for this specific route. It adds the onsen ryokan experience that Osaka → Hiroshima → Kyoto is missing, costs the least transit time of all three options, and Mt Fuji views are a genuine bonus. This is the itinerary's current Day 9–10 recommendation.
伊豆
Izu Peninsula
Shizuoka · South of Tokyo · ~2.5–3.5h from Kyoto
✓ Second Trip
Why You Should Go
  • Far less crowded than Hakone — genuinely off the international tourist radar despite being close to Tokyo
  • Outstanding onsen towns: Shuzenji (traditional bamboo grove hot springs), Atami (historic grand onsen resort), Shimoda (southern tip, where Commodore Perry opened Japan in 1854)
  • Beautiful Pacific coastline with dramatic sea cliffs, fishing villages, and black sand beaches — a completely different aesthetic from the rest of Japan's inland cities
  • Wasabi farms and fresh wasabi cuisine unique to the Izu region (Amagi area)
  • Saphir Odoriko — one of Japan's most stylish new express trains, running from Tokyo through Izu
  • The Invisible Tourist specifically recommends Izu/Shuzenji over Hakone for 2026 due to Hakone's crowd issues
The Trade-offs
  • Routing problem: Izu Peninsula extends south from Tokyo — from Kyoto, you'd go to Mishima or Atami (~1h 50m), then travel south into Izu (~1–2h more). Leaving Izu for Tokyo means backtracking north. This adds ~2–3h of redundant transit vs Hakone.
  • The deepest/most interesting parts of Izu (Shimoda, Dogashima) require 2.5–3h from Tokyo — more time-consuming than Hakone
  • Less "packaged" than Hakone — requires more research to get the most out of it; can disappoint if you end up in Atami without exploring further south
  • Not covered by JR Pass for most of the peninsula (Izu Kyuko line requires separate tickets)
Verdict: Genuinely beautiful and less crowded — but the routing from Kyoto → Izu → Tokyo involves backtracking that Hakone doesn't. Best saved for a second trip when you're based in Tokyo and can do a standalone 2-night Izu escape. On a first trip arriving from Kyoto, Hakone wins on logistics.
Mid-Trip Stop: Head-to-Head Comparison (Days 9–10 slot, arriving from Kyoto)
Hakone Izu Peninsula Takayama
Transit from Kyoto ~2h · On Shinkansen line ~2.5–3.5h · Slight detour south ~3h · Detour north via Nagoya
Transit onward to Tokyo ~35 min – 1.5h ~1.5–3h (backtrack north) ~5h via Nagoya
Routing efficiency ✓✓ Zero detour ✓ Slight backtrack – Significant detour
Onsen / Ryokan quality ✓✓ World-class ✓✓ Excellent, less crowded ✓ Good but not the focus
Mt Fuji views ✓ Close (weather permitting) ✓ From the sea (Atami) – Not visible
Cultural uniqueness ✓ Art + nature + onsen ✓ Coastal + onsen + history ✓✓ Edo-period, most distinct
Crowd level High (popular resort) Low (under-visited) Medium (manageable)
JR Pass coverage Odawara only · Hakone Pass €40 Mishima/Atami only · local extras ✓✓ Mostly covered
Best for Art lovers, Fuji dreamers, onsen first-timers Crowd-averse, coastal lovers, second Japan trip Traditional Japan purists, rural culture seekers
SRC

Research & Itinerary Sources

This guide synthesizes recommendations from multiple expert sources. Here's what each one emphasizes — and how our itinerary compares to the consensus.

日
Japonismo.com
Japan specialist · 20+ years · Spanish-language authority
Their 2-week Classic Route
Day 1 Tokyo → Kyoto (Shinkansen) · Days 2–3 Fushimi Inari & Arashiyama · Day 4 Hiroshima + Miyajima day trip · Day 5 Nara & Osaka · Day 6 Himeji Castle · Day 7 Travel to Takayama · Day 8 Takayama · Day 9 Shirakawa-go + Kanazawa · Day 10 Kanazawa · Days 11–13 Back to Tokyo · Day 14 Departure
Key insight: Japonismo initially had two separate routes — one with Miyajima+Hiroshima and one with Takayama+Shirakawa-go — but after 20 years of reader feedback, combined both into one. They also strongly recommend Kanazawa and Himeji Castle as worthy additions.
英
National Geographic + Western Experts
NatGeo · The Invisible Tourist · NeverEndingFootsteps
Western Consensus Route
The Invisible Tourist recommends 5 nights Tokyo, 4 nights Kyoto, 2 nights Hiroshima, 3 nights Osaka — emphasizing depth over breadth. National Geographic highlights Kanazawa (Edo-era samurai district + Kenroku-en garden) and Koyasan (Buddhist mountain monastery) as the most-overlooked stops for first-timers.
Key insight: For 2026, The Invisible Tourist recommends avoiding Hakone (overcrowded) and switching to Shuzenji Onsen or Izu Peninsula for Fuji-area onsen experiences.
案
Japan-Guide.com + Japan Highlights
Oldest online Japan resource · Research-based travel advisors
Their Emphasis
Japan Highlights strongly recommends adding Kanazawa alongside Takayama for a richer "Japanese Alps corridor" experience. Japan-Guide notes that the JR Pass has increased in price significantly since 2023 (~¥50,000 for 14 days) and now requires careful math vs individual tickets depending on your exact route.
Key insight: Koyasan (sacred Buddhist mountain near Osaka, 90-min by train) is consistently highlighted as a unique overnight temple-stay experience — perfect for adding to an Osaka base, and not in our current plan.
Destination Consensus Across Sources
Destination In Our Trip Japonismo NatGeo Invisible Tourist Notes
Tokyo ✓ 5 nights ✓ ✓ ✓ 5 nights Universal must. 5 nights is ideal.
Kyoto ✓ 3 nights ✓ ✓ ✓ 4 nights Most recommend 4+ nights. 3 is tight but doable.
Osaka ✓ 3 nights ✓ ✓ ✓ 3 nights Universal. Often used as base for Kansai day trips.
Hiroshima + Miyajima ✓ Day 14 ✓ ✓ ✓ 2 nights Universal. Invisible Tourist recommends 2 nights.
Nara ✓ Day trip ✓ Day trip ✓ ✓ Day trip Universal consensus day trip from Osaka or Kyoto.
Takayama ✓ 2 nights ✓ ✓ ✓ Recommended by all. Best paired with Shirakawa-go.
Kanazawa — Not included ✓ ✓ Optional Edo samurai city, often paired with Takayama. Ideal for 16–17 day trips.
Koyasan — Not included Optional ✓ Optional Sacred Buddhist mountain near Osaka. Exceptional overnight temple stay.
Himeji — Not included ✓ Half-day Mention Optional Japan's finest original castle. Easy half-day from Osaka on the way to Hiroshima.
If You Had 16–17 Days…

Add 2 nights to extend Osaka-base with: Kanazawa (bullet train from Osaka via Tsuruga, ~2.5h, new Hokuriku Shinkansen extension opened 2024) + Koyasan overnight temple stay, or consider a night at Shirakawa-go from Takayama.

JR Pass 2025/26 Value Check

Since the October 2023 price increase, the 14-day JR Pass (~¥50,000/€313) is still worth it for this itinerary — the Tokyo-Hiroshima Shinkansen alone is ~€113 each way. However, calculate your specific routes first at japan-guide.com before purchasing, especially if Takayama is removed from your plan.

What to Eat

Tokyo
Ichiran RamenSolo-booth tonkotsu ramen — iconic Tokyo experience~€7.50
Wagyu YakinikuGrill premium Japanese beef tableside in Shibuya or Ginza~€25–50
Tsukiji SushiUltra-fresh nigiri at the outer market stalls~€12.50
Harajuku CrepeOverstuffed street crepe with matcha, strawberry & cream~€4.40
Takayama + Nara
Hida Beef SkewerLocally raised wagyu — excellent value vs Tokyo/Osaka~€9.40
Takayama RamenSoy-sauce broth, lighter and more nuanced than Tokyo style~€5.65
Kakinoha-zushiNara's pressed sushi wrapped in persimmon leaves — ancient recipe~€7.50
Mitarashi DangoGrilled mochi balls with sweet-soy glaze from Takayama stalls~€1.90
Kyoto / Osaka
Kaiseki RyoriKyoto's multi-course seasonal haute cuisine — splurge at least once~€50+
TakoyakiOsaka's octopus balls with mayo and bonito flakes — essential~€3.75
OkonomiyakiOsaka-style mixed vs Hiroshima layered versions~€6.25
Matcha EverythingKyoto is matcha HQ — lattes, parfaits, wagashi sweets~€3.15–5
Hiroshima / Miyajima
Hiroshima OkonomiyakiLayered style with noodles — try at Okonomi-mura food hall~€6.25
Grilled OystersMiyajima's enormous briny sea oysters — grilled over coals~€3.15
Momiji ManjuMaple leaf-shaped cakes filled with red bean or chocolate~€1.25
Anago MeshiSea eel over rice — a Miyajima specialty since 1903~€12.50
04

When to Visit

Japan has four beautifully distinct seasons — and the timing of your trip dramatically changes the experience. Here's a region-by-region, month-by-month breakdown to help you decide.

🌸
Spring
March — May
The most iconic season. Cherry blossoms (sakura) sweep north from late March — Tokyo and Kyoto hit peak bloom around late March–early April. Temperatures are pleasant (15–22°C / 59–72°F). April is widely considered the best month in Japan.
Tokyo8–22°C
Kyoto / Osaka10–24°C
Takayama (Alps)2–18°C
  • +Cherry blossoms & plum blossoms
  • +Ideal walking weather
  • +Festivals: Hanami parties, Kyoto Geiko dances
  • –Peak crowds & peak hotel prices
  • –Golden Week (late Apr–early May): domestic travel surge
☀️
Summer
June — August
Hot, humid, and rainy. The tsuyu (rainy season) runs June–mid-July. After it clears, temperatures hit 30–35°C with high humidity. Typhoon season begins in August. Hokkaido is a pleasant exception — mild and cool all summer.
Tokyo24–35°C
Kyoto / Osaka26–36°C
Sapporo (Hokkaido)17–25°C
  • +Vibrant summer festivals (Gion Matsuri July, Obon Aug)
  • +Fewer international tourists (except Obon)
  • +Perfect for Hokkaido + flower fields
  • –Honshu is very hot & humid (exhausting for sightseeing)
  • –Typhoon risk Aug–Sept; rainy season June–July
Best Season
🍁
Autumn
September — November
Peak season alongside spring. Brilliant red and gold autumn leaves (koyo) sweep south from Hokkaido in September, reaching Kyoto by mid-to-late November. Temperatures drop to a perfect 15–25°C. October is the sweet spot: post-typhoon, pre-crowds.
Tokyo15–28°C
Kyoto / Osaka13–26°C
Takayama (Alps)5–20°C
  • +Koyo: stunning red maple & ginkgo colors
  • +Comfortable walking temperatures
  • +Best food season (mushrooms, chestnuts, sake)
  • –Kyoto in Nov is extremely crowded
  • –Book 4–6 months ahead for accommodation
❄️
Winter
December — February
Cold but beautiful. Tokyo and Kyoto get cold (3–10°C) with occasional frost. Takayama gets significant snow. Hokkaido is world-class ski territory. Fewer tourists at major sights, excellent illuminations, and the best onsen season.
Tokyo2–12°C
Kyoto / Osaka3–12°C
Sapporo (Hokkaido)-8–0°C
  • +Fewer crowds at temples & shrines
  • +Sapporo Snow Festival (Feb) — spectacular
  • +Onsen are at their best in the cold
  • –Short daylight hours (sunset ~4:30 PM Dec)
  • –New Year (Dec 29–Jan 4): many sites close
Average High Temperatures by Month (°C) — Key Cities
Jan Feb Mar Apr May Jun Jul Aug Sep Oct Nov Dec
🗼 Tokyo 10 11 14 19 23 26 30 31 28 23 17 12
⛩️ Kyoto 10 11 15 21 26 29 34 35 30 24 18 12
🏔️ Takayama 4 5 10 17 21 25 28 29 24 18 12 6
🌿 Hiroshima 10 11 14 20 24 27 31 33 29 23 17 12
🦌 Sapporo (ref.) -1 0 5 12 18 22 25 26 22 15 7 2
■ Spring (Best) ■ Summer (Hot) ■ Autumn (Best) ■ Winter ■ Sapporo summer sweet spot
⚠️ Dates to Avoid or Prepare For
Dec 29–Jan 4 New Year: major domestic travel, many sites close
Late Apr–May 5 Golden Week: domestic surge, hotels 2× price
Mid-August Obon: domestic travel peak, rural attractions busy
Late Aug–Oct Typhoon season — check forecasts before travel days
✓ Best Times for This Specific Itinerary
Late March–April Cherry blossoms in all 6 cities. Book 4–6 months ahead.
Mid-May Post-Golden Week: lush green, mild, fewer crowds
October Sweet spot. Post-typhoon, pre-koyo crowds. Clear skies.
Mid-Nov Kyoto koyo peak. Breathtaking but very crowded.
IC

IC Cards & Transit

An IC card is the single most important item you'll set up on arrival in Japan. Think of it as a tap-to-pay transit card that also works at convenience stores, vending machines, taxis, and hundreds of retail shops — making cash almost unnecessary for day-to-day spending.

🚃
All trains & buses
JR local lines, subways, private railways, buses, ferries, airport express trains. One tap — no fumbling for tickets.
🏪
Konbinis & shops
Works at 7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson, Ministop, McDonald's, Starbucks, Uniqlo, and thousands more.
🥤
Vending machines
Tap the reader on almost any vending machine in Japan. Cold drinks, hot coffee, snacks — instant payment.
🚕
Taxis & lockers
Most taxis accept IC payment. Also works for coin lockers at train stations — essential for luggage-free sightseeing days.
The Main IC Cards
🐧
Suica
JR East · Tokyo & surroundings
Recommended

The most widely accepted IC card in Japan and the easiest to add to Apple Wallet as an Express Transit card (no Face ID needed to pay). Issued by JR East and ubiquitous in Tokyo.

Works everywhere Suica / PASMO / ICOCA are accepted — which is essentially all of Japan.

🔵
PASMO
Tokyo Metro & private railways

Tokyo's subway-focused IC card, issued by the Tokyo Metro group. Interchangeable with Suica for all practical purposes. Also available as a digital card (PASMO PASSPORT) designed for tourists.

Can also be loaded to iPhone Wallet.

🦦
ICOCA
JR West · Kansai region

JR West's card, most commonly obtained in Kansai (Kyoto, Osaka, Nara). The capybara-duck mascot ("ICOCA Duck") is a fan favourite. Works everywhere that other IC cards work throughout Japan.

Physical only — no digital wallet option currently.

🔄
All Major IC Cards Are Cross-Compatible

Suica, PASMO, ICOCA, manaca, nimoca, Kitaca, TOICA, and Hayakaken all work on each other's networks. You do not need to switch cards when moving between Tokyo and Osaka. The card you get at Narita on Day 1 will work seamlessly in Kyoto on Day 9 and Hiroshima on Day 14.

📱
Digital — iPhone Wallet (Recommended)
iPhone 8 or later (iOS 16+) can add a Suica or PASMO card directly to Apple Wallet. It acts as an Express Transit card — meaning you tap your locked phone to the reader, no Face ID or screen wake needed. Faster than a physical card.
✓ Add in Apple Wallet → (+) → Transit Card → Japan
✓ Top up via Apple Pay (any linked credit card)
✓ Works even when phone battery is very low (Express mode reserves charge)
✓ Android users: Google Pay + Suica app works similarly
– US-issued Amex cards sometimes have issues topping up — have a Visa/Mastercard ready
💳
Physical Card
Available at any JR ticket machine at Narita, Haneda, and all major stations. Requires a €3.25 deposit (refunded when you return the card). Top up at any station machine or konbini.
✓ Works for everyone regardless of phone type
✓ Easy to lend to travel companions
✓ Fully refundable at JR East counters on departure
– Physical card stock has been limited due to chip shortage — see tourist versions below
Tourist-Specific Versions (2025/26)
Welcome Suica
JR East — Narita & Haneda airports
A 28-day, non-refundable Suica designed specifically for tourists. No €3.25 deposit. Available at JR East Travel Service Centers at Narita (T1/T2) and Haneda. Comes pre-loaded with €6.25 or €12.50.

Note: Due to ongoing semiconductor shortages, availability has been intermittent since 2022. Check JR East's website before travel — and if unavailable, use digital Suica in Apple/Google Wallet instead. It's faster anyway.
PASMO PASSPORT
Tokyo Metro group — airports & major stations
Similar to Welcome Suica — 28-day, no deposit, non-refundable. Available at PASMO PASSPORT vending machines at Narita (T1/T2/T3), Haneda (Int'l terminal), and major Tokyo Metro stations. Works identically to a standard PASMO.

Note: Also subject to chip shortage disruptions. If unavailable, go digital. The app-based PASMO works on iPhone and Android.
💡 Pro tip: Add €19–31 at the start of each city stay. Many IC card readers will show your balance after tapping — top up before it drops below €3.25 to avoid being stuck at a turnstile. Most station machines and all konbini registers can top up any IC card.
05

Essential Chains & Shopping

Japan's chain culture is exceptional — not the sad compromise it often is elsewhere. These stores and restaurants are institutions, and knowing how to use them will improve your trip daily.

🏪 The Big Three Konbinis (Convenience Stores)

Japanese convenience stores are a genuine cultural achievement. Open 24/7, they sell fresh food made daily, hot meals, great coffee, ATMs (the only reliable ones for foreign cards), postal services, event tickets, and more. Visit multiple times per day — it's completely normal.

7
7-Eleven
セブン-イレブン · ~21,000 stores
The largest chain and widely considered to have the best food quality. House brand 7Premium is genuinely excellent. Their ATMs (Seven Bank) reliably accept all foreign Visa/Mastercard/Amex — the go-to for cash withdrawal in Japan.
⭐ Best onigiri selection (try Salmon with cream cheese)
⭐ 7-Café drip coffee machine: €0.70 for excellent hot coffee
⭐ Best sandwiches and premium egg salad sandwich (tamago sando)
⭐ Gold Blend noodle cups and 7Premium snacks
FaM
FamilyMart
ファミリーマート · ~16,500 stores
The trendiest of the three, with frequent limited-edition collaborations with anime, games, and fashion brands. Famous for their Famichiki fried chicken (arguably Japan's best fast-food chicken) and their soft-serve ice cream. Best hot foods section.
⭐ Famichiki — seasoned fried chicken, €1.45. Get two.
⭐ Soft cream (soft-serve): seasonal flavours are excellent
⭐ Collab merch — fun limited items with popular IP
⭐ Best selection of chilled desserts (pudding, mochi)
L
Lawson
ローソン · ~14,500 stores
Known for premium sweets and the best baked goods counter. Has a sub-brand, Natural Lawson, focused on healthier options. Also home to Karaage-kun (bite-size fried chicken balls). Best dessert section of the three.
⭐ Karaage-kun fried chicken balls (regular & cheese flavour)
⭐ Premium sweets — Uchi Café series, roll cakes
⭐ Best bread/pastry selection (croissants, melon pan)
⭐ Machikado Kitchen: freshly prepared hot meals at select stores
🍜 Affordable Dining Chains Worth Knowing
🍛
CoCo Ichibanya カレーハウス CoCo壱番屋
~1,200 locations · €4.50–8
Japan's most beloved curry chain. Choose your spice level (1–10, where 5 is already very hot), rice amount, and toppings. Customizable to an almost absurd degree — over a trillion possible combinations. Ideal for solo dining or when the group can't agree. Vegetarian options available.
🍚
Yoshinoya, Matsuya & Sukiya 吉野家 / 松屋 / すき家
~1,200 / ~1,000 / ~1,900 locations · €2.50–4.50
The holy trinity of gyudon (beef bowl) chains — hot simmered beef over steamed rice, ready in under a minute. Yoshinoya is the original (est. 1899) and the most iconic. Matsuya skews slightly higher quality with miso soup included and a wider menu. Sukiya is Japan's largest chain by locations — the most family-friendly, with more topping options (cheese, kimchi, natto). All under €4. Order at a ticket vending machine, sit at the counter, eat fast.
🍔
Mos Burger モスバーガー
~1,300 locations · €3.75–7
Japan's finest domestic burger chain, founded 1972. Uses fresh, never-frozen patties and house-made sauces. Signature item: the Teriyaki Burger and the Mos Burger (meat sauce + lettuce). Messier than western fast food — that's a feature, not a bug. Often has seasonal specials with Japanese ingredients.
🍣
Kura Sushi くら寿司
~550 locations · €0.75–1.50 per plate
The most tech-forward kaiten (revolving) sushi chain. Plates arrive via enclosed conveyor belts (hygienically sealed), you order via touchscreen tablet, and every 5 plates earns a gacha capsule toy game. No additives, no artificial colours. The tuna, salmon, and egg sushi are excellent for the price. Also try Sushiro and Hamazushi for variety.
🍜
Ichiran Ramen 一蘭
Major cities · €6–10
The solo-dining ramen experience: individual wooden booths with bamboo curtains, personal order forms to customize your tonkotsu broth (richness, noodle firmness, spice, garlic). No speaking required. Quietly perfect for introverts and jet-lagged travellers at any hour. Also try Ippudo for a livelier atmosphere.
🍱
Tenya & Marugame Seimen 天や · 丸亀製麺
Nationwide · €2.50–4.50
Tenya = fresh tempura rice bowls (tendon) for under €3.75. Marugame Seimen = hand-pulled udon made in front of you, choose your broth and toppings cafeteria-style. Both are excellent value and quintessentially Japanese. Marugame's kakiage udon (crispy vegetable tempura on cold udon) is one of the great cheap meals in Japan.
🍵
Soba Chains: Fuji Soba & Yude Taro 富士そば · ゆで太郎
Tokyo-heavy · €2.50–5
Standing-only (tachi-kui) soba noodle bars — pay at the machine, collect your ticket, eat at the counter in under 10 minutes. Fuji Soba is the beloved Tokyo chain open 24h at busy stations. Yude Taro grinds buckwheat in-house for better flavour. Hot kake soba (plain broth, noodles, spring onion) for €2.50. The definitive Tokyo commuter experience — and one of the best cheap meals in Japan.
🥩
Yakiniku Like 焼肉ライク
~170 locations · €7–14
Solo yakiniku (Japanese BBQ) chain — each seat has its own individual grill, exhaust hood, and ordering tablet. Designed for dining alone without the awkwardness of a full BBQ restaurant. Wagyu and domestic beef options. Brilliant concept: you grill small portions of marinated beef, pork, and kalbi at your own pace, served with rice and miso. One of the few places to eat proper yakiniku without booking or overspending.
🍗
Torikizoku 鳥貴族
~650 locations (Osaka-heavy) · €2–2.50 per skewer
The people's yakitori (grilled chicken skewer) izakaya — every single item on the menu costs exactly €2–2.50. Chicken thigh, cartilage, liver, tsukune (meatball), leek, and quail egg skewers, all grilled over charcoal. A full satisfying dinner with drinks costs €12–16. Founded in Osaka and more concentrated in Kansai, but expanding. The best way to experience an izakaya atmosphere without breaking the budget.
🥟
Gyoza no Ohsho 餃子の王将
~740 locations · €3–8
Japan's definitive gyoza (pan-fried dumpling) chain, beloved for over 50 years. Six pan-fried pork gyoza for €3.75 — crispy-bottomed, juicy inside, ideal with the house garlic soy dipping sauce. Also serves fried rice, ramen, and mapo tofu. The gyoza set with fried rice and soup is one of the best value meals in Japan at ~€5. Heavy Kansai presence; a staple for locals in Osaka and Kyoto. The regional rival in Tokyo is Harajuku Gyoza Lou.
🛍️ Retail Essentials
🎪
Don Quijote (Donki)
ドン・キホーテ · ~700 locations · Open late (many 24h)
Japan's legendary chaotic discount megastore. Floor-to-ceiling stacking, jingle playing on loop, everything at once: electronics, cosmetics, food, anime merch, costumes, appliances, alcohol, and luxury goods all in the same store. The greatest impulse-buying environment ever created. Tax-free counter for tourists on purchases over €31.
Best for: snacks to take home, skincare (cheap vs department stores), souvenirs, nighttime chaos, themed character goods, booze
💴
Daiso / Seria / Can★Do
100-yen shops · ~4,500 locations (Daiso alone)
Japan's ¥100 shops (~€0.65) carry items of inexplicably high quality for the price: kitchenware, stationery, sewing kits, travel accessories, party supplies, seasonal décor. Seria is considered the premium tier with better design. Daiso has the most variety. Bring an empty bag — you will fill it.
Best buys: travel pouches, chopstick sets, phone stands, face masks, wrapping paper, seasonal stationery
🎵
Tower Records Japan
タワーレコード · Shibuya flagship + nationwide
While Tower Records collapsed globally, it thrives in Japan — physical media still sells here. The Shibuya flagship (8 floors) is the most famous record store in the world. Carries J-pop, city pop, jazz, classical, anime soundtracks, K-pop, and extensive international sections. Also stocks books, merch, and art goods. A cultural landmark.
Best for: city pop vinyl, anime soundtracks, J-pop idol releases, exclusive Japanese pressings, browsing for hours
👗
Uniqlo & GU
ユニクロ / ジーユー · Nationwide
Japan Uniqlo stores have a wider range than Western locations — including Japan-exclusive colour ways, collab lines, and seasonal Japanese patterns. Japanese Heattech and AIRism are notably better quality. GU is Uniqlo's cheaper sub-brand (think: fashion-forward H&M prices). Also accepts IC cards.
Best for: Japan-exclusive patterns, summer festival wear (yukata), quality basics at lower-than-overseas prices
📚
Book-Off / Mandarake
Used books, manga, games, retro goods
Book-Off is Japan's massive used media chain — manga volumes for ¥100, used games, retro electronics, and secondhand clothing at some locations. Mandarake (Nakano Broadway, Tokyo) is the legendary otaku specialty store for rare figures, vintage manga, and anime goods.
Best for: ¥100 manga volumes, retro games, anime figures, affordable Japanese books to practice reading
💊
Matsumoto Kiyoshi / Ainz & Tulpe
マツモトキヨシ · Drug & beauty stores
Japan's pharmacy chains double as lifestyle beauty stores. Matsukiyo (the yellow stores) is the most common; Ainz & Tulpe is the upscale version with excellent lighting and curated Japanese cosmetics. Tax-free on €31+ cosmetics purchases. Essential for skincare, Hada Labo toner, sunscreen, face masks, and stomach medicine (Seirogan).
Best buys: Hada Labo HA lotion, Shiseido Anessa sunscreen, Pola BA series, Seirogan stomach tablets
04

Getting Around

Between Cities
🚄
Shinkansen (Bullet Train)
Buy a 14-day JR Pass before leaving home — covers all major routes plus the Miyajima ferry. Absolutely essential for this itinerary.
JR Pass 14-day: ~€313 · Buy before travel · Covers all Shinkansen + Hiroshima ferry
🏔️
Hakone Free Pass (2-day)
Covers all internal Hakone transport: Tozan train, ropeway, cable car, Lake Ashi pirate ship ferry, and Hakone-Yumoto buses. Buy at Odakyu counters in Tokyo or Odawara. JR Pass only covers Shinkansen to Odawara.
Hakone Free Pass 2-day: ~€40 · Worth it — you will use every mode
🦌
Osaka → Nara (Day Trip)
Take Kintetsu Nara Line from Osaka-Namba (direct, ~40 min). Faster than JR. Not covered by JR Pass but very cheap.
Kintetsu: ~€4.25 each way
⛴️
Ferry to Miyajima
10-minute JR ferry from Miyajimaguchi pier. Completely free with your JR Pass!
Free with JR Pass · Runs every 10–15 min
Within Cities
🚇
Suica / IC Card (Metro)
Load at any station. Get one at Kansai International Airport (KIX) on arrival — IC cards work nationwide from Day 1.
~€1.25–2.20 per ride · Top up at any station
🚌
Local Bus
Kyoto's bus network connects all temples. In Nara, everything is walkable from the station (~15 min). Takayama is a compact walking town.
Kyoto 1-day bus pass: €4.40
🚕
Taxi / GO App
Download the GO app for on-demand taxis. Great for late nights or carrying bags. Avoid in Tokyo peak hours — use the subway instead.
Start fare ~€4.60 · Rises per km
05

Budget Breakdown

✈️ Flights (Return, approx.)~€740
🏨 Accommodation (15 nights avg. €75/night)~€1,120
🚄 JR Pass 14-day~€313
🍜 Food & Drink (~€37/day)~€520
🎟️ Activities & Entrance Fees~€185
🛍️ Shopping & Souvenirs~€185
🚇 Local Transport (Suica, Nara train, taxis)~€130

* Estimates in EUR (~¥160/€). Nara Kintetsu train not on JR Pass (~€8.50 round trip). Book peak seasons 3–6 months in advance.

Estimated Total (Per Person)
~€3,100
Including flights · 15 days · Mid-range
  • Without flights~€2,360
  • Daily spend (excl. flights)~€170/day
  • Budget traveler daily~€75–93
  • Mid-range (this plan)~€130–190/day
  • Luxury traveler daily€230–370+
  • Nara day trip extra cost~€8.50 (Kintetsu)
  • Pocket WiFi / SIM card (14d)~€28–46
  • Travel insurance (recommended)~€56–93
06

Insider Tips

🎟️
Book Online Before You Go

Teamlab Planets, Tokyo Skytree, Kaiyukan Aquarium, and the Samurai Ninja Museum all sell out or have very long walk-in queues. Book at least 2–3 weeks in advance via official websites.

🌅
Rise Early, Beat the Crowds

Fushimi Inari, Arashiyama Bamboo Grove, and Itsukushima Shrine are mobbed by 9 AM. Arrive before 7 AM for a meditative, near-empty experience. Completely worth the alarm clock.

🦌
Nara's Deer Will Rob You

The 1,200 deer in Nara Park are sacred and shameless. Hide food inside bags — they will headbutt and bite your clothing. Buy official deer crackers (shika senbei, ~€1.25) from designated vendors.

🌊
Check the Tide at Miyajima

The torii gate only "floats" at high tide. Check tide tables before Day 5 — aim for high tide in the morning (shrine appears to float) and low tide in the afternoon (walk out and stand beneath the gate). The JMA Japan tide forecast website is free and accurate.

💴
Japan Is Very Cash-Friendly

Many small restaurants, temples, and market stalls are cash only. Withdraw yen at 7-Eleven ATMs (accepts all foreign cards, 24h). Always carry ¥5,000–10,000 in your wallet.

🧳
Use Luggage Forwarding (Takkyubin)

Ship your suitcase between hotels for ~€9–16. Life-changing especially on the Hakone→Tokyo day and the KIX arrival day. Book the day before at any convenience store — luggage arrives next day.

Japan 2-Week Travel Guide · 6 Cities · 15 Days · Created by Claude · Prices in EUR (approx. ¥160/€) · Always verify hours & book tickets before travel
